article_title: GDB 17 Brings Hardware Shadow-Stack Debugging and AArch64 Guarded Control Stacks as the 17.2 Maintenance Release Lands
summary: Accurate, fully-sourced GDB 17 release report; every version/date specific verified against sourceware.org primary announcements and corroborated by LWN. Only finding is the expected non-blocking sourceware.org off-allowlist warning (canonical GNU toolchain home).

content_preview.title: GDB 17 Brings Hardware Shadow-Stack Debugging and AArch64 Guarded Control Stacks as the 17.2 Maintenance Release Lands
content_preview.summary: The GNU Debugger's 17 series adds support for debugging CET shadow stacks and AArch64 Guarded Control Stacks, with version 17.2 shipping in May 2026 as the current release.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The GNU Debugger, the decades-old command-line debugger at the foundation of much of the open-source toolchain, has reached its 17 series. The current release, GDB 17.2, shipped on May 10, 2026 as a maintenance update, according to the [GDB project's news page](https://sourceware.org/gdb/news/). It builds on GDB 17.1, released December 20, 2025, which introduced the series' substantive new capabilities, per the [GDB 17.1 release announcement](https://sourceware.org/pipermail/gdb-ann...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured, appropriately scoped News piece (824 words, within the 400-1200 range). Clear separation of the current 17.2 maintenance release from the feature-bearing 17.1 release, with a measured Analysis section that frames GDB's adaptation to hardware control-flow-integrity and editor-driven (DAP) workflows without overstatement.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 snapshots read from disk (uncompressed sha256 of each matches manifest.json). source-0 (sourceware.org GDB 17.1 announcement, status 200): confirms 17.1 released Sat Dec 20 2025 by Joel Brobecker; x86-64 CET shadow stack support; Debugging Linux programs that use AArch64 Guarded Control Stacks; GDB record now supports rv64gc; info threads -stopped/-running; info sharedlibrary full memory range on Linux/FreeBSD; built-in TLS fallback on x86_64/aarch64/ppc64/s390x/riscv with GLIBC or MUSL; set riscv numeric-register-names; Alpha target descriptions; DAP 'completions' request; emoji warning/cross-mark prefix (configurable); Python <3.4 removed; stabs and a.out/dbx deprecated. Every body specific maps verbatim. source-1 (sourceware.org GDB 17.2 announcement, status 200): confirms 17.2 released Sun May 10 2026 by Joel Brobecker, 'brings the following fixes and enhancements over GDB 17.1'; exactly 14 PR fixes enumerated, matching the article's 'resolving 14 reported issues'. source-2 (sourceware.org GDB news page, status 200): 'May 10th, 2026: GDB 17.2 Released! The latest version of GDB, version 17.2, is available for download' (listed first/latest), and 'December 20th, 2025: GDB 17.1 Released!' — corroborates current-release framing. source-3 (LWN, status 200): 'GDB 17.1 released', changes include shadow-stack support, AArch64 Guarded Control Stacks, configurable emoji, DAP completions, Python <3.4 removal, stabs deprecation, signed by Joel Brobecker — all LWN attributions in the article hold. source-4 (Wikipedia GNU Debugger, status 200): GDB first written by Richard Stallman in 1986 as part of the GNU system, released under the GNU General Public License, source-level debugger for Unix-like systems, stable release 17.2 — all four 'according to Wikipedia' claims hold.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: No misstatement of which release introduced which feature: the substantive 17-series capabilities are correctly attributed to the GDB 17.1 announcement, while 17.2 is correctly described as the current maintenance release resolving 14 issues. Version/date specifics (17.2 = May 10 2026 current; 17.1 = Dec 20 2025) are cited to the sourceware.org primary announcements and independently corroborated by LWN and the GDB news page. No hallucinated quotes (the article contains no direct quotations in quote marks). No fabricated specifics detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Analysis section is interpretive but grounded and avoids sensationalism. No AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: No prior GDB / GNU Debugger coverage in the May or June 2026 archives. Genuinely new topic.
concerns: Three sourceware.org URLs are off the source allowlist, producing the automated script's single non-blocking warning. sourceware.org is the canonical home of the GNU toolchain and the sole authoritative source for GDB release announcements; the load-bearing version/date claims are additionally corroborated by LWN (on-allowlist) and the GDB news page. This is a provenance/tooling note, not a reader-facing factual error, so no corrections record is filed. The allowlist was not modified.
recommendations: Maintainers may consider adding sourceware.org to config/source_allowlist.txt given its standing as the primary GNU toolchain source, but this is out of scope for this review and was intentionally not changed.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: APPROVE. A clean, accurate, fully-verified release report. Every claim, date, version, and technical specific traces to a cited source and was confirmed against the committed snapshots, with primary-source citation for the version specifics (Rule 9 satisfied) and independent corroboration. The only finding is an expected, non-blocking off-allowlist warning that does not warrant a correction. The script's mechanical APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS verdict (triggered solely by that allowlist warning) is overridden to APPROVE because no corrections note could honestly inform readers of any factual issue.
